videos.title: 盈利是假象？百济神州：一款药撑起3000亿市值，危险才刚开始 |  Beone  | ONC | Zanubrutinib |泽布替尼 | 国产创新药 |
videos.transcript: 百济神州 堪称中国创新药的标杆级选手 过去十几年烧光了500亿资金 顶着连年巨亏的压力 硬生生的在全球创新药市场 杀出了一条血路 2025年 百济神州这家公司 终于实现了首次盈利 结束了长达十几年的亏损年代 一时间行业内外一片喝彩 按理说这应该是里程碑 是股价起飞的开始 但是在这份盈利的财报公布之后 资本市场非但没有买账 股价反而迎来了一波跳水 港股单日跌幅超过9% a股美股相继迎来了暴跌 很多投资者都懵了 明明扭亏为盈 营收大涨了40% 为什么市场反而用脚投票 这里先给出一个结论 就是百济神州并非没有问题 而是问题刚刚开始 所以今天这期视频 我们就来聊一聊百济神州的投资价值 以及它背后的风险 hello大家好 这里是美股博士 每周分享美股干货 片尾我的实盘千万记得看到最后 今天这期视频分成三个部分 第一部分 百济神州是靠什么来赚钱的 第二部分 百济神州当前最大的隐忧是什么 第三部分 如何看待百济神州的投资价值 首先就是百济神州 这家公司是靠什么实现扭亏为盈的 百济神州能够实现盈利 最大的功臣 毫无悬念 就是旗下的核心BTK抑制剂Zanubrutinib 商品名是百悦泽 可以说没有Zanubrutinib 就没有百济神州的今天盈利 这款药是公司的利润奶牛 也是整个公司业绩的绝对支柱 2025年全年 Zanubrutinib全球销售额狂揽39亿美金 同比增长了49% 同类产品全球销售额第一 凭借一己之力 撑起了公司超七成的营收 直接把公司从亏损的泥潭里拉了出来 实现了自我造血的关键跨越 你可以这么理解 这家公司基本上就是靠这一款药 养活了整个公司 这就听起来有点耳熟了是不是 对这就是典型的单爆款公司结构 你比如说莫德纳，BioNTech这些等等 像一些早期的生物药企都是这样子的 一般来说 当所有公司的盈利 基本都压在一款产品上之后 公司并不是更安全 反而是更脆弱了 为什么会这么说 因为Zanubrutinib看似高增长的背后 其实已经浮现出了一些隐忧 而且这两大隐忧正在逐步逼近 这也是资本市场最担心的核心问题之一 首先 第一大隐忧就是增速大幅放缓 熟悉创新药行业的朋友都知道 一款重磅单品的增长 往往是“高速放量—— 平稳增长——增速下滑的”生命周期 Zanubrutinib前几年的增速堪称恐怖 2324两年连续实现翻倍增长 靠着全球市场的快速渗透 撑起了公司营收的高速扩张 但是到了2025年 从之前翻倍的增长 回落到了48.8% 尤其是盈利核心市场美国 增速从2024年的107.5%跌至45.5% 这背后并不是说百济神州的运营不利 而是市场份额见顶之后的必然结果 为什么会这样 原因很简单 就是市场份额马上就打满了 Zanubrutinib在BTK赛道 已经是全球第一了 美国核心市场的渗透率接近天花板 接下来会发生什么 所有的爆款药 基本都会经历同一个阶段 “高速增长——渗透完成 ——增速下滑 ——价格竞争” 也就是说 最轻松最赚钱的阶段基本已经过去了 第二重引诱就是竞争加剧 竞品通过降价来维持市场 创新药行业从来都是红海市场 Zanubrutinib的火爆 必然引来各路竞争对手的追捧 老牌PTK抑制剂Ibrutinib （口误：伊布替尼） 为了守住市场份额 在美国的第二轮医保谈判中 直接降价38% 2026年正式实施 以价换量的策略 直接影响到了Zanubrutinib的定价空间 和市场份额 除此之外 我我在先前视频里介绍的礼来（LLY）的 新一代BTK抑制剂Pirtobrutinib快速上市 针对的是使用过Zanubrutinib等药物 出现耐药的患者 这款药物临床数据亮眼 也是国产PD-1中的头部选手 适应症覆盖了多个实体瘤 但是2025年全年 百泽安的全球销售额仅为7.37亿美金 同比增长了19% 而且体量只有Zanubrutinib的1/5 增速也远低于Zanubrutinib 虽然获批国家多 但是核心适应症缺乏竞争力 欧美市场被Keytruda（来自默沙东） Opdivo（来自施贵宝）等老牌的PD-1药物牢牢占据 很难实现突破 国内PD-1市场内卷严重 医保降价后利润微薄 同质化竞争激烈 根本无法承接Zanubrutinib的增长缺口 当前市场 对于百济神州最期待的产品 就是BCL2抑制剂 Sonrotoclax 商品名是百悦达 这是百济神州布局的第二款 血液瘤核心药物 被视为 最有可能承接Zanubrutinib增长的产品 公司还重点推广 Zanubrutinib+Sonrotoclax的组合疗法 试图打造新的盈利增长点 这款药已经在中国获批上市了 美国上市申请也在审批中 2026年有望获批 看似前途光明 但是当前的商业化并不及预期 在国内上市之后放量缓慢 获批后仅数百名患者使用 短期只贡献了温和的营收 其次 全球BCL2抑制剂市场竞争同样激烈 同类多款产品已经上市 市场格局基本定型 Sonrotoclax想要突围难度极大 百济神州旗下 还有多款处于临床阶段的在研药物 涉及了血液瘤实体瘤 自身免疫系统疾病等等多个领域 看似管线丰富 但是绝大多数 都处于临床早期 距离商业化还有很长一段路 短期无法贡献营收 也就是说 站在当前这个时间节点 百济神州过去十几年的研发 真正实现商业化的 只有Zanubrutinib一款产品 其他管线要么是盈利微薄 要么持续烧钱投入 形成了一家公司靠一款药 撑起了全部营收的极端格局 对于资本市场而言 投资者可以接受创新药企短期烧钱 但是长期看不到管线兑现的希望 尤其是在核心产品接近见顶的情况下 第二曲线的落地速度 直接决定了这家公司的未来 你可以这样理解 现在的百济神州是一种很典型的情况 当下靠一款药赚钱 未来要靠一堆可能性 这种单一产品依赖 对于创新药企究竟有多危险呢 首先就是业绩稳定性差 完全绑定一款产品的生命周期 创新药企的生命有限 一旦核心产品出现增速下滑 竞争失利 专利到期临床副作用等任何一个问题 整个公司的营收和利润 都会直接受到影响 没有任何缓冲空间 就像Zanubrutinib增长放缓 百济神州的整体业绩立马承压 资本市场直接用股价大幅下跌回应 这就是最好的证明 其次创新药企的早期阶段 往往投入研发性价比失衡 抗风险能力极弱 2025年 百济神州的研发投入超过21亿美金 同比增长10% 巨额的研发投入 本应该打造多元的产品矩阵 分散风险 但是目前来看 似乎只有这一款药物实现了商业化 其余管线迟迟无法兑现价值 相当于是把所有的鸡蛋放在一个篮子里 一旦篮子摔了 就会满盘皆输 对比我先前给大家介绍的GSK 旗下多款重磅单品并行 营收结构均衡 抗风险能力强 百济神州的业务模式 显然还没有达到 成熟药企的发展逻辑 最后就是长期发展缺乏支撑 估值逻辑当前相对脆弱 资本市场给创新药的估值 不仅看当下的盈利 还要看未来的增长潜力 单一产品依赖的药企 估值完全绑定核心产品的销售预期 一旦市场对核心产品的增长预期下跌 公司估值就会快速缩水 所以 百济神州这家公司 很大程度上 就是靠Zanubrutinib的高增长预期支撑 如今增长放缓叠加问题暴露 估值重构在所难免 这也是股价大跌的核心原因之一 看到这可能有些朋友会说 很多创新药企都是靠单一产品突围 你把百济神州这一问题放大 是不是对这家公司有一点不公平 没错在当前这个发展阶段 靠单一产品放量没有问题 但是百济神州这家公司 当前正在接近 或者已经接近了盈利增长的拐点 即将步入成熟期 站在百济神州当前这个时间节点 想要让股价进一步起飞的催化剂是 第二增长曲线已经显现 但是百济神州 当前并没有摆脱单一产品依赖 也就是说依然靠一条腿走路 我们都知道 两条腿走路才能走得更稳 所以说百济神州在当前这个阶段 一直靠一条腿蹦跶 显然不符合资本市场对这家公司的预期 所以说这也是百济神州这家公司 当前面临的最大问题 说完了百济神州这家公司的问题 接下来我们进入第三部分 谈一谈百济神州这家公司的投资价值 百济神州的未来 它并不取决于过去 而是取决于未来两到三年 核心只有一件事 最理想的状态下 就是再做出一款 类似Zanubrutinib的超级大爆款产品 如果这样不行 那做出几款 年收入突破10亿美金的小爆款单品 也是可以接受的 如果这些事情成立的话 那公司就（大概率）能顺利完成转型 如果不成立 那公司可能还要停留在 “一款药撑起一家公司”的格局 所以说百济神州这次股价暴跌 它并不是失败 而是资本市场 对于这家公司的一次重新估值 过去十几年 百济神州这家公司证明了一件事 就是中国的药企 可以做出全球的超级大单品 但是接下来 这家公司需要证明一件更难的事情 能不能从一家单一产品依赖的公司 转型成为一家多产品的公司 这两件事情的难度 完全不在同一个量级 咱们这个频道的常客BNTX和MRNA 已经证明了 这件事情的难度绝对不低 所以这次百济神州股价下跌 并不是在否定过去 而是提前定价了未来的不确定性 核心判断就是 百济神州已经证明了他可以成功一次 但是还没有证明他能连续成功 当前的资本市场 只会为第二件事情买单 对于投资者而言 我们要谨慎的看待这家创新药龙头 既不要盲目吹捧它的盈利光环 也不要一味否定它的行业价值 资本市场的股价下跌 不否认它的过去 只是在担忧它的未来 接下来1-3年是百济神州最重要的阶段 Sonrotoclax的商业化 在研管线的临床突破 以及海外市场放量 每一个节点都影响着这家公司的命运 百济神州这家公司 能否摆脱单一产品依赖 找到新的产品增长引擎 将决定这家公司能够成为创新药龙头 还是仅仅停留在一款药的辉煌 最后做一个小结 盈利从来都不是创新药企的终点 而是新的起点 当下的百济神州依然面临三大难题 第一就是核心产品增长放缓 第二就是单一产品依赖 第三就是第二增长曲线还没有显现 这三大问题 并不是短期能够一蹴而就解决的 需要公司加快管线的商业化 优化业务布局 打造多元的盈利矩阵 中国的创新药行业发展道阻且长 像百济神州这样的头部药企 基本上可以说在一定程度上 承载了行业的希望 我们期待他能突破当下的困局 实现多元化的发展 也期待更多的国产创新药 能够走出国门 造福全世界的患者 好了这就是关于百济神州我想说的 接下来我们进入实盘公布 hello大家好 本周我的回报率是-2.67% 2022年3月以来账户回报率是146.86% 仓位方面没有调整 只有每周定投的微软 昨天LLY宣布 他的三靶点减肥药retatrutide 取得了积极的顶线结果 在使用这款药40周之后 受试者糖化血红蛋白 也就是衡量这个患者 （近期）血糖控制情况的指数 平均下降了1.7%到2.0个百分点 最高剂量组 患者的平均体重下降了16.8% 而且 目前尚未观察体重下降出现平台期 这项实验 是一项为期40周的随机双盲对照实验 旨在比较retatrutide与安慰剂 在2型糖尿病成人患者中的疗效 和安全性 相较于先前动辄五六十周的临床实验 这次40周的实验时间显然比较短 所以从这项实验中观察到 受试者未出现体重下降平台期 就很值得关注了 也就是说 随着实验时间的延长 患者的体重很有可能继续下降 除此之外 retatrutide 还在多项关键的心血管风险因素中 显现出了积极的临床实验结果 包括了低密度脂蛋白 甘油三酯以及血压等等 和其他基于肠促胰素的疗法 在临床实验中观察到的不良数据一致 接受了retatrutide的受试者中 最常见的不良反应 就为恶心腹泻和呕吐 而且主要发生在剂量递增（口误）期间 也就是说 剂量越大 副作用的发生率越高 说完了LLY的新产品 大家难免想要知道 NOVO的产品进展如何 上个月公布了二期临床实验数据 24周最大减重19.7% 看起来疗效不错 但是有两点值得关注 第一这不是完整的数据 没有停药率等等的安全细节 第二 这只是二期临床实验数据的结果 而上面提到的LLY 已经完成了三期临床实验 接下来就要申报上市了 所以说LLY的三靶点减肥药 可以说领先了诺和诺德 一个完整的临床周期大概是两年以上 好了从后台的数据我可以看到 有接近一半的观众 是通过手机观看我的节目 我知道可能有一点不方便 但是如果你已经看到这里 非常非常希望你能点一下置顶 盈透证券的开户链接 只要简单的点一下 然后关闭就可以 那点击不会对您收取任何费用 但是这是支持我这个频道 最最最最重要的方式 我也会持续的分享更有价值的内容 来回馈大家 非常感谢 好了 这就是今天这期视频的全部内容 我们下期视频不见不散

videos.title: 40歲生日，我決定不再”努力“了
videos.transcript: OK. Today I turned 40. Yeah, the day I'm recording this video is my lunar birthday. And on my 40th birthday, I made a decision. I'm going to stop hustling so hard. Now, I know that might sound like I've made enough money, and I'm ready to lie flat. Actually, it's the exact opposite. It's one of the hardest things I've ever done in my life. Because for the past 40 years, I realized the only thing I know how to do is be tense and push hard. But now I realize, if I want to unlock the next chapter of my life, I need something completely new, and that's relaxation. What? Relaxation? That's it? I know what you're thinking, but hear me out. Most people get relaxation completely wrong. Relaxing isn't lying flat, it's not just having fun, and it's definitely not a reward you only earn after you succeed. Instead, it's the prerequisite for breaking through your bottlenecks and opening the next door. And the reason I'm making it my life's focus at 40 comes down to three things: a point of confusion, a scar on my wrist, and a book. What's your name? Ray. Ray, I'm Ray. Thank you, thanks. So that confusion came from a quote by Naval. He said, if you want to get rich, spend your time learning, earning, or relaxing, outsource or ignore everything else. Learning and earning, I get that. I've been doing those two things for the last decade, and it's what I always preach on this channel and in my community. But why spend time relaxing? I could never really wrap my head around it systematically. Then, earlier this year, I randomly read a really old book called Psycho-Cybernetics, translated as Xin Li Kong Zhi Shu. After reading it, it cleared up my confusion about Naval's philosophy on relaxing. So today, I want to use my own experiences to talk about why, if we want to unlock life's next chapter, every one of us needs to learn how to relax. I'll just have a hot mocha, easy to sip. You guys always see me wearing this Apple Watch in my videos. Honestly, I don't really like wearing watches. I wear it mostly to cover up a massive scar on my left wrist. Hey, don't overthink it, I didn't cut myself or anything. But this scar, in a way, is a microcosm of my tense, wound-up life. This is my birthday dinner. When I was a freshman in high school, my mom opened a small restaurant outside the coal mine where we lived. It was a tiny business, but grueling work. Whenever I went home for the weekend, I'd go help out, often working late into the night, and then we'd push our tricycle back home. One day, I think the gas tank ran out or something, I forget the exact details. I just remember it was just me and my mom, and a bunch of customers were waiting, getting super impatient. So I took a kitchen knife to chop some wood and help start a fire. But I accidentally hacked right into my left wrist. Blood just started spraying everywhere. I could literally see the bone. I was terrified. But I didn't dare tell my mom, afraid I'd interrupt her work. I just told her I cut my hand by accident, grabbed my wrist, and ran to a small clinic. The doctor there stopped the bleeding and gave me a bunch of stitches. The clinic was super sketchy, and the doctor's skills were pretty limited. So when the wound healed, the nerves were messed up. To this day, the area around my thumb is totally numb. I can't feel a thing there, but if you touch the scar, it hurts like crazy and gets super tingly. So if anyone ever gets into a fight with me, all they have to do is hit my left wrist hard, and they can take me down instantly. When I got back to school with this injury and people asked what happened, I started lying. I said I was playing around with a kitchen knife and accidentally slashed myself while hacking at stuff. And this lie had a few different versions. Like sometimes, depending on who I was talking to, I'd say I got slashed in a fight when I was a kid. It made me sound tough. I told these lies so many times that eventually, I kind of believed them myself. It became my default answer whenever anyone asked. I didn't even realize I was lying anymore. So why did I lie? Originally, it was just because I didn't want people looking down on me. Other kids went on vacations during their time off, while I spent my holidays working for survival, helping out in a tiny, run-down restaurant, and hacking myself in a moment of panic. Later on, I just wanted to hide the scar and everything it represented deep down inside, to give myself a goal and the strength to change my destiny against all odds. So I just kept holding my breath, pushing through, trying to force my fate to change through sheer hustle. My sophomore year of high school, I worked insanely hard. I studied until 1 or 2 AM every night and woke up at 5. I shot up to third place in my science track, and kept my grades top-tier all the way through senior year. Everyone thought I was going to a top university. But I bombed the entrance exam and only got into a second-tier college. But then a magical thing happened when I went to this second-tier college. Without the pressure of the gaokao, without everyone's expectations and eyes on me, I actually started to let loose and be myself. I started reading, listening to English podcasts, even working part-time and starting mini-businesses. That period was the fastest time of growth and transformation in my entire life. In a way, it made me who I am today. OK, OK, I gotta stop here, or I could talk about myself all day. So why am I telling you all this? What does it have to do with relaxing? Well, after reading Psycho-Cybernetics, and looking back at this and many similar experiences in my life, I noticed a pattern with myself. Whenever I'm tense and wound up, I usually mess things up. To use a trendy phrase, the harder I try, the worse I fail. And it's only when I relax that good things actually happen. I've been saying this phrase a lot lately. It's "Good things only happen when you are relaxed." Why is that? In the book, the author talks about a success mechanism. In English, it's called the success mechanism. He says that built into every one of our subconscious minds is an auto-navigation system, just like a missile's guidance system. It automatically corrects your course and guides you toward your target. You don't need to tell it how to take every single step. You just set the target and let it run. It's basically what we mean when we say "manifesting your desires." There's a quote in The Alchemist that goes, "When you want something, all the universe conspires in helping you to achieve it." I'm sure most people have had experiences like this. But here's the catch: this system has one condition to operate. You have to be relaxed enough. When you're tense, anxious, and desperately trying to micromanage every single step, your conscious mind forces its way in and tries to hijack the auto-pilot. And the result is, you just jam the whole system up. It's like walking while carrying a full glass of water, trying not to spill it. The more you think, "Don't spill," and the harder you stare at the glass, the more you end up spilling. But if you ignore it, stop caring, and just relax, the water stays right in the cup. The book refers to this as the creative mechanism. It gets locked down by your forebrain. So the more you want it, the worse you perform. Specifically, your inability to relax holds you back in these four dimensions. We often think that pressure comes from motivation. I pushed myself to study hard to crush the gaokao, driven by this core belief. Counterintuitively though, that understanding might be completely wrong. The book mentions an experiment: when animals learn about a new environment without too much pressure, they form a very broad cognitive map, exploring all sorts of possibilities and gaining a comprehensive understanding of their surroundings. But if the pressure is super high, that cognitive map shrinks drastically. They only focus on the most urgent thing right in front of them, and go completely blind to everything else. "Pressure retards learning." Pressure directly slows down your learning. And it doesn't just make you slow; it makes your learning narrow. The higher the pressure, the more inefficient and narrow your learning becomes. This makes you inflexible. When you're finally facing a real challenge, your performance naturally drops. That explains why I messed up my college entrance exam. Because I didn't just fail from being too nervous and jamming my success mechanism. Fundamentally, learning under all that pressure made my scope way too narrow. I only knew how to take tests, and only specific kinds of tests. My whole way of thinking was completely rigid. So when faced with a real challenge like the Gaokao, I had zero flexibility and no ability to adapt. Looking at the bigger picture, this also explains a phenomenon a lot of people feel but can't quite put into words. Why is it that "small-town test machines" like me, who didn't come from a great background, did well in school and were great at taking tests, but once we enter society, we end up performing worse than kids who grew up in more relaxed environments, even if their grades weren't as good? We used to think it was just because they had more resources or better connections. But this book made me realize there's a deeper reason: we thought we were learning better, but actually, we were learning much narrower. They grew up in a more relaxed environment, so their cognitive map is way broader than ours. Meanwhile, those small-town test machines are much more likely to face high-pressure situations. So at the end of the day, "happy learning" actually makes sense. And our high-pressure background has been affecting our entire life trajectory. Now, this isn't to say that studying and working hard under massive pressure can't bring you success. But the secret is, if you want to unlock even greater success, you need to give yourself room to breathe and learn how to relax. I know what you're gonna say: "Easy for you to say! You can't just relax on command." "What if my circumstances don't allow me to relax?" "Rui, when you were a small-town test machine, you couldn't relax either, right?" "Now that you're well-off, it's easy for you to preach." Yeah, I don't deny that. For a lot of people in crisis mode, it's impossible to completely let go. But we have to understand that "relaxing" is actually a spectrum, not just a single state. And that brings me to my second point: your inability to relax is an overreaction. It turns you into a reactor in life, rather than an actor. The book says humans are fundamentally actors, not reactors. We aren't just ships drifting in the wind. We have goals and we have direction. If your reaction to your environment isn't pushing your goals forward, or is even holding you back, what you really should do is just not react. The worrying, the anxiety, and the stressing we do every day—that's exactly this kind of useless reaction. They don't help you move forward; they only keep you stuck. What you really need to do is stop reacting. For example, the book points out that the reason most of us worry, get anxious, and panic essentially stems from a really terrible mental habit: you always feel like you should be doing a million things right now. While you're working on Thing A, you suddenly remember Thing B isn't done, you want to do Thing C, and Thing D can't be delayed either. And the next passage from the author is something I absolutely love. He says that even on your busiest day, every single task can only come to you one by one. It has to. No matter how many problems, tasks, or pressures you have, they can only line up single file and come at you one at a time. That's just an objective law of reality. So when you have a million things swirling in your head, you're actually violating that reality. Because we overreact, we end up procrastinating, avoiding things, overthinking, worrying, or even getting crushed by the pressure. We fail to act as the one in control, deciding what to focus on in this exact moment, and that leads to us doing a terrible job at everything. That's why so many productivity books emphasize single-tasking and push back against multitasking. And when books teach us mindfulness and focusing on the present, they're essentially just following this exact same rule of taking things one step at a time. They're telling us to relax and stop overreacting. It's just that they usually focus more on the spiritual or wellness side without really explaining the underlying mechanics. But this book figured it all out and explained the logic perfectly over half a century ago. And this applies not just to a specific task, but to our entire life journey. We always say "life is a journey," but honestly, a lot of times we don't really grasp what that actually means. In recent years, a lot of books have been preaching "enjoy the process" and downplaying the importance of goals. Early this year on our channel, we read a book called Tiny Experiments, which even goes as far as saying you shouldn't set goals at all, you should set experiments. But that totally contradicts another popular narrative. There are endless "success" books and productivity guides teaching you how to set goals, make plans, and break down execution. So which one is actually right? Well, Psycho-Cybernetics gives a crystal clear answer: they're both right, you've just messed up the ratio. The book says humans are naturally goal-oriented animals. Without a goal, you wouldn't even start. So how could there even be a process? Therefore, goals are absolutely necessary. But once you've set that goal, you should actually be in "journey mode" for the vast majority of the time. In journey mode, you are an actor. You decide exactly where to step, and you even get to decide to enjoy the scenery along the way. But when you're only staring at the goal, you end up overreacting. Trying too hard turns you into a reactor, and you end up losing your footing. What you really should do is spend most of your time watching the road right under your feet, and only occasionally look up to make sure you're still heading the right way. Yet so many of us do the exact opposite. We spend too little time in journey mode and stare way too hard at the goal. The more you stare, the more the pressure pushes you from being an actor to a reactor, and you end up moving slower by trying to rush. The heavier your goals are, the more you need to learn how to relax. This is incredibly relevant to my life right now. I've mentioned before that I've achieved relative financial and time freedom, and now I only work on things I love, aiming for big goals. But I found that with such massive goals and so many projects, I'm constantly trying to solve every single problem in front of me as quickly as possible. It leaves me feeling suffocated. Sometimes I get so overwhelmed by the tasks— wanting to do everything right now but ending up doing nothing at all— that I forget to focus on the process and even want to just give up. And worse, it makes me beat myself up, thinking I'm just not capable enough. And that brings us to the next major way pressure ruins our progress. My 40th birthday is over. Let's go to the studio. Let's see if that security cat is still there. The other day on Twitter, I saw a highly debated clip of the billionaire Marc Andreessen. It sparked a ton of controversy. In an interview, he said he rarely self-reflects. He's even read over 400 biographies of great entrepreneurs, and found that almost none of them engage in self-reflection. "You don't have any levels of introspection." "Yes, zero. As little as possible." "Why?" "Move forward. Go. Yeah, I, I don't know." "I just find people who dwell on the past get stuck in the past. It's, it's just, it's a real problem and it's a, it's a problem at work and it's a problem at home." He criticized self-reflection as a modern invention, a psychological and philosophical movement driven by guilt. Great figures of the past didn't do this. They only looked outward and forward to build great things, instead of sitting around criticizing themselves and doubting themselves while living in the past. As soon as he said that, the internet blew up. His critics and his supporters started arguing fiercely, and Marc himself didn't let it go either— he kept getting into endless debates with his haters right there on Twitter. Now, I know his statement is definitely extreme, but after I watched it, I thought, doesn't this perfectly align with the point I've been trying to make? If we apply this idea of "self-reflection" to the success mechanism we've been talking about today, it's basically an error-correction mechanism. There's nothing inherently wrong with correcting errors. Your success mechanism needs it, just like a missile has to constantly adjust its trajectory to hit the target. But the book immediately follows up with a crucial prerequisite: for this error correction to actually work, it has to be subconscious, automatic and spontaneous. Meaning, the correction has to happen at a subconscious level. It has to be automatic and unprompted. Any conscious effort to engage in self-criticism and self- correction is fundamentally over-correcting, and it does more harm than good. Why? For example, if you're giving a speech on stage, your eyes will naturally scan the audience's faces. And if someone frowns, you might subconsciously slow down your pace, or explain things in a different way. You won't even realize you're doing it. That's course correction at the subconscious level. It runs quietly in the background, highly accurate, and very efficient. But if, during your speech, someone suddenly stands up and boos you, or the voice of self-criticism in your head gets loud enough, then this correction signal gets dragged from the subconscious right into your conscious mind. Your focus is completely shattered. You start doubting and second-guessing yourself, your system overloads, and you panic. The exact same signal helps you when it's subconscious, but becomes pure interference when it hits the conscious level. That's exactly how self-criticism works. Of course you need to correct mistakes and improve, but you should let your subconscious handle that process— auto-correcting and auto-adjusting. When you drag it into your conscious mind, turning it into "Why am I so bad?" or "I messed up again," or "Am I just a failure?"—then you're no longer correcting, you're attacking yourself. Your success mechanism isn't being fixed, it's being suppressed. So when we talk about relaxing, it really just means letting your conscious mind step back and letting your subconscious take the wheel. Being tense is just your conscious mind fighting for control. When your success mechanism is suppressed, the most obvious symptom is that you're afraid to be yourself. Why emphasize "being yourself"? Because deep down, every single one of us has a true self, and a natural instinct to express it. True success is finding and unleashing that authentic version of you, and answering that ultimate question: "Who am I?" But excessive self-criticism actively crushes that expression. I often say that as a YouTuber, succeeding is actually really simple: you just need to take how you express yourself off-camera and bring that exact same energy on-camera. Off-camera, when nobody's around, you can make goofy faces in the mirror, sing freely, crack jokes, totally full of confidence. But the second the camera rolls, you become a totally different person. You can tangibly feel yourself holding back. But notice that most of the time, what's suppressing you isn't the audience, it's you. It's your own conscious mind. When you drop that self-consciousness and hit a state of flow, that's when you achieve your maximum self-expression, and that is your ultimate potential. And this state of flow is the deepest form of relaxation. Very few people can actually pull it off, myself included. But for those who can, it's almost impossible for them not to succeed. Now, the stage or camera example is specific, but it's also universal, because we're all performing on the stage of our own lives. A lot of the time, we don't even realize we're suppressing ourselves. And the more you suppress yourself, refusing to release your unique personality and nature, the further away you get from the success you want. That's why, at 40 years old, I decided to pursue just being "loose," to capture that feeling of true relaxation. I get the logic, but so often I still can't help it. I still tense up and consciously criticize myself. So that leads to what I want to share next—the most important lesson I learned from Psycho-Cybernetics: and that is saying goodbye to the straw man from your past. Going back to that Marc Andreessen interview, did you notice? He said self-criticism is a guilt-driven psychological philosophy, and people trapped in it usually live in the past. Well, this book mentions the exact same thing. The author says, "Don't fight the straw man out of the past." Stop wrestling with the ghost of yesterday. A lot of the tension and self-criticism you feel right now isn't reflecting anything happening in the present; it's reacting to a threat that hasn't existed for years. It's like how the college entrance exams are long gone, but so many of us still dream about them, and most of the time, it's a nightmare. I often talk with my friends about how so many people have been stuck in survival mode for so long. Always the reactor, always on edge. But you're not in that environment anymore. You are safe now. Yet your system is still running on that old code. We don't know how to play in exploration mode. We don't know how to go back to being an actor, because we've been tense for so long we think it's the only way to live. And that scar I mentioned at the start of this video? That's my straw man. I used lies to bury it in the deepest corner. And by avoiding it, I couldn't ever say goodbye to it. It pops up from time to time to stop you from being your true self. So I'm putting it out there today, because I finally want to say goodbye to it. Your straw man might look different from mine. It might be that one time you were rejected as a kid, or a moment of public humiliation, or the fear you internalized after being punished, or the constant hint that you just weren't good enough. But whatever it is, it belongs to the past. It has no place in your present. In The Courage to Be Disliked, which we read before, there's a concept a lot of people struggle to accept: that trauma doesn't exist. It doesn't mean pain isn't real. It means your current reaction isn't being forced on you by that past event. You have a choice. This is exactly what Psycho-Cybernetics is saying too. The book makes it very clear: when faced with a fake problem, the best reaction is actually no reaction. Reacting makes you a reactor—it makes you tense. Not reacting makes you the actor—it makes you relaxed. So when we talk about learning to relax, it essentially means learning not to react to non-existent problems, to problems we've blown completely out of proportion, to emotions and environments that can hurt us. Not reacting. Capturing that relaxation, letting the straw man fall, letting your success mechanism genuinely run for you again, achieving maximum self-expression, and unlocking the next chapter of your life. This is why, at 40, I decided to stop pushing so hard, and made learning to relax a major life priority. Naval says there are three things in life: learn, earn, relax. I've spent the last 30 years doing the first two, and now it's time to start the third. Because "Good things only happen when you are relaxed."

videos.title: ✨【投资TALK君1412期】最磨人的跌法！AI智能体入口是谁？展望智能体的未来✨20260326#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
videos.summary:
**核心要点：**
- 市场对特朗普讲话脱敏，关注点转向霍尔摩兹海峡通行和停战信号，战争事件导致市场阴跌。
- 苹果Siri将对其他AI助手开放，不赌单一大模型，而是看好各模型在专有领域的市场空间，Siri可能成为个人智能体的流量入口，通过与其他智能体的交流帮助用户解决问题。

- 苹果估值处于过去5年中位数水平，与谷歌类似。今年大科技股普遍下跌，苹果表现相对较好，下跌7%，而微软下跌24%表现最差。

videos.title: SCHD Will Never Be the Same After This
videos.transcript: The most popular dividend ETF in America just removed 22 stocks and it replaced them with 25 new ones. And here's something that you need to know. The stocks that they removed are up an average of 6.7% this year. And the stocks that they just added, they are down an average of 9.4%. That's a 16 percentage point swing in the wrong direction. SCHD is an extremely popular fund and they happen to be managing nearly a hundred billion dollars. And they just happen to sell some of their biggest winners year to date and in return they bought a basket of potential losers and they didn't get rid of these companies because they're failing and it's not because the dividends were in trouble in any way, but it's because something is forcing this fund to make this trade. And when you understand what that something is, it changes how you think about whether SCHD actually protects your money or if it's just another lemming just running right off the cliff. Let me show you why this matters to every single person that's watching this video. So, last year SCHD made the opposite move. It loaded up on energy. It took it from 12% of the fund all the way up to 21%. That was the single biggest sector bet that SCHD had ever made and it worked because the energy sector just ripped and SCHD had crushed the broader market and energy was that engine that was behind all of it. So now the fund turns around and it cuts that same position nearly in half. Almost eight billion in energy stocks rotating out of a 99 billion dollar fund. And as a point of reference, Valero is up over 45% this year and it just got removed. 22 stocks are now completely out and 25 new ones are in its place. And this was not a simple little trim. This was a full complete overhaul. Now, here's where it starts to get a little personal. If you're one of the millions of people sitting on bonds or treasuries right now collecting 4.39% completely risk free waiting for the right time to rotate back into something like SCHD, well, you might find this a little bit confusing because the stocks that they just added to this fund haven't really been on a hot streak. Just look at United Health, one of the largest additions by market cap is down over 17% this year. And the stocks that just left, they were carrying double digit gains. The fund just sold some of its biggest winners and it replaced them with names that haven't really proven anything yet this year. But if you already own SCHD, well, you happen to just watch the fund sell the stocks that were driving all of those bigger returns. All of that appreciation, a huge chunk of it just walked out the door. So, I'm guessing that there's two camps of people watching this right now. People sitting on the sidelines waiting to buy and people holding SCHD wondering if they should have sold. And of course, one of you is going to be really happy six months from now and one of you is going to wish that you maybe would have watched this video a little more carefully. At the end of the day, this is just a simple math problem that nobody knew needed to be solved. And yeah, I get it, that may sound a little cryptic, but I'll I'll explain why. SCHD doesn't even have a portfolio manager. It tracks the Dow Jones US Dividend 100 Index and every year that index runs its entire portfolio through a set of quality screens. No human decides what stays or goes. It's really just a simple formula. To even qualify for the fund, a company has to have paid dividends for at least 10 consecutive years. Then every qualifying company gets scored on four different things. That happens to be return on equity, cash flow relative to its debt, current dividend yield and a five-year dividend growth rate. The top 100 companies happen to make the cut. Everyone else, well, they're just they're out. It doesn't matter how well your stock is performing. If your quality score slips below that top 100, you're gone. And that is exactly what happened to the energy stocks. And here's the part that's a little bit counterintuitive because energy stocks performed so well, their stock prices went way up, which means their dividend yields went way down. And that's how dividends work because it's a set dollar amount per stock. So, if the stock price goes up, the dividend rate goes down. So, the same strong performance that made them winners in your portfolio is what made them score lower on these quality screens and that's why it's counterintuitive. I mean, think about that for a second. The fund sold its winners not because they had failed, but because they became so successful that it changed their math in a negative way. Now, here's a detail a lot of people might miss and that's that the fund didn't abandon energy entirely. Conoco Phillips and Chevron are still in the top five holdings and Devon Energy was actually just added and it happens to be up over 27% this year. So, the fund kept the highest quality energy names and it cut the ones whose scores just happened to slip a little. My point is that's not exactly panic selling. That's the system that's doing exactly what it was designed to do. The bigger question is, are you on board with that? And another question to consider, is this version of SCHD, the one that just restructured itself, worth buying right now? And if you already own it, should you be a little worried? Let me start with the honest answer for the people that are on the sidelines looking to get in. The 10-year Treasury yield is sitting at around 4.39% right now. SCHD yields at about 3 and 1/2%. So, on paper the government is paying you more for zero risk and the only way that Treasury yield comes down is if the Fed begins to drop its rates. And as of right now, they're projecting just one cut this year. One. And that's pretty optimistic at best because inflation doesn't seem to be slowing down. The oil crisis is pushing energy costs higher every day and until that resolves, the Fed really has no reason to move aggressively in dropping rates. So, if the risk of recession increases, then bonds look like a much better short-term play. I mean, you're getting 4.39% guaranteed while SCHD carries the market risk with a lower starting yield. So, for someone looking to deploy new capital today, the math doesn't favor SCHD over treasuries, at least not in the short term and that's just our current reality. Now, here's the other side. If you already own SCHD, this is a completely different conversation. SCHD isn't just a yield play. Over the last 10 years, SCHD has returned over 232% including dividends that were reinvested. The S&P 500 returned about 272% over that same time period. So, yes, the S&P 500 did outperform it on total return. So, if you had put a hundred thousand dollars into SCHD a decade ago with dividends reinvested, that is worth roughly 332,000 dollars today. So, your 3 and 1/2% yield isn't being calculated on that original 100,000 dollars anymore. It's now being calculated on 332,000 dollars. That massive bump now becomes over 11,000 dollars a year in dividends without adding a single dollar. Compare that to the 3,500 dollars that you'd be collecting in year one. The yield percentage barely even changed, but the dollars almost tripled because the base had tripled. And this is something that you will never get with bonds and why so many people want to figure out the best time to rotate back into something like SCHD. And the funny thing is is that the Fed tells you nearly exactly when the right time will be. Now, once again, coming back to the reality of our global situation, energy has a tendency to come crashing down once an oil crisis is averted. And of course, I did show this in my prior video and we've seen it happen repeatedly. But the resolution could be six months away or it could be a year or several years or it could be weeks. We just don't know. It all depends on how the war plays out and whether Iran continues to be a factor. So, in the meantime, SCHD just rebuilt its portfolio with companies that score higher on quality fundamentals. If energy corrects and the broader market stabilizes, the names SCHD just added are the type that tend to recover really well. The methodology she proved, it adapts. It doesn't hold on to yesterday's winners because of sentiment. It reruns the quality filter and it rebuilds. So, in a roundabout way, shedding some of these energy stocks could be a brilliant move. But I also think that they have a few months of solid growth that SCHD will completely miss out on. So, if you're holding, the reconstitution actually did what it was supposed to do. It positioned the fund for what comes next, not for what already happened. Two different audiences, two different answers. So, if you're sitting in treasuries collecting 4.39%, there's really no rush to move into SCHD right now. There just isn't. But if you're already in SCHD and you're a little bit nervous about this reconstitution, well, the fund just did exactly what you're paying it to do. It sold the stocks that scored lower on quality and replaced them with ones that scored higher. That discipline is what keeps this fund relevant year after year. And long-term, I I'm pretty sure it's going to serve you very well. If this helped you think about SCHD a little bit differently than before, please press the like button so my channel can continue to grow. And as always, thank you for watching. We'll see you next time.

videos.title: 大科技为何狂跌？美股何时反弹？ | 猴哥财经 | 美股 | 投资 | 股票 | AI
videos.summary:
**核心要点：**
- 美股科技股近期大幅下跌，纳斯达克指数较去年高点下跌超10%，进入回调区域，微软、Meta等大科技股票跌幅近30%，进入技术性熊市。
- 与此同时，能源、工业、消费者必需品等旧经济板块逆势上涨，市场出现结构性资金流动，价值轮动正在发生。
- 价值轮动可能由战争、油价、通胀预期等因素触发，但大科技股基本面依然健康，预计轮动将是短期的，一旦宏观环境改善，成长股有望快速反弹。
- 未来成长股反弹可能需要油价回落、AI商业变现加速或估值进一步修正等条件触发，预计伊朗战争若在未来3个月内缓和，成长股可能迎来快速反弹。
- 建议投资者保持谨慎，不要因短期价值轮动而动摇长期持仓逻辑，对于大科技股，可以考虑在技术位分批次建仓，长期看好其基本面和AI布局。

videos.title: 五小时法则：成为富豪的习惯 | 全球顶尖成功人士遵守的成功法则
videos.transcript: 为什么我们每天努力工作 可是最后的成就却千差万别呢？ 答案其实并不在于我们在工作时间有多拼命 而是在于我们如何利用好空闲的时间 今天查理就来为大家介绍  很多成功人士都在使用的五小时法则 利用好一周中这五个小时 就能让我们在生活中 取得事半功倍的成就 简单来说 五小时法则指的是无论有多忙 每天至少将一个小时 或者每周五个小时 保留给自己用来学习 这个法则是美国开国三杰之一 本杰明·富兰克林 (Benjamin Franklin) 的时间管理原则 作为著名的物理学家和发明家 他最早提出了电荷守恒定律 他还是美国独立战争时重要的领导人之一 也是美国首位邮政总局局长 可以说 富兰克林的人生就是许多杰出人物的生命总和 那么一个人到底怎样在一生中有限的时间里 做出那么多伟大的事的呢？ 在富兰克林的相关著作中 有人挖掘出了富兰克林成年以后 一直遵循的时间管理原则 他坚持在每个工作日 周一到周五 专门空出 1 个小时进行学习 学习的内容包含各个领域 同时他还会制定个人成长目标 并会追踪和反思自己的实践效果 这样下来一周就有 5 个小时 后来人们也将这个法则称为 富兰克林的 5 小时法则 这个原则看上去极其简单 但实际上坚持下来的难度非常高 五小时法则的核心在于 给日程留白 而不在于每天 1 小时这个量化标准 但即使是保留少于一小时的空白时间 在这个信息高速运转的互联网时代 我们也很难做到 查理曾经看过一个有趣的研究 在一个对 200 多名白手起家的百万富翁 进行为期五年的研究当中发现 许多成功人士平常都不看电视 而且 令人印象深刻的是 其中有 86% 的人 都会在空闲的时间进行阅读 还有 63% 的人表示  他们在早上通勤时会听播客 股神巴菲特把 80% 的时间花在阅读和思考上 如果你看过他的每周日程表 你就会发现他甚至还有专门的理发时间 周二是理发日 这就是巴菲特 创造世界上最成功的商业记录之一的原因 他有很多时间思考 而且在整个职业生涯中 他都是这样去规划自己的时间的 为什么一家有数十万员工公司的首席执行官 会有这么多空闲时间 而我们的日程却排得满满的呢？ 其实利用好空闲时间 并不是让自己陷入忙碌 而是要创造闲暇时间 让自己有空学习 来超越生活中其他没有意义的事 并获得观察问题的新视角 把无意义的事情剔除 给生活留白 可以让我们有时间认真考虑 自己想要学习的东西 这样才能提升自己 而不是漫无目的的做事却毫无进步 既然我们一周只抽出五小时的时间学习 那就一定要学会高效输入和输出 推崇这个法则的成功人士 往往会分成三个部分来进行 首先是阅读 埃隆 马斯克 (Elon Musk) 就说过 他是通过阅读学会了如何制造火箭 并因此创立了 SpaceX 阿里巴巴创始人马云说 除了扩展你的知识 阅读可以让你有一个好的开端 这往往是你的同龄人无法获得的 与其他人相比 读者更有可能了解其他行业的战略和战术 大多数的我们作为普通人 就算没有创造商业帝国的梦想 也可以在长期的阅读习惯中获得收益 所以就算你不能保证每天阅读一个小时 或者更长时间 也不要轻易放弃 就像前面提到的 阅读一个小时不是硬性的指标 你可以选择抽出 20 到 30 分钟来开始阅读 生产力专家乔恩塞 马多克斯 (Choncé Maddox) 写道 成功人士阅读不是秘密 据说普通百万富翁每月阅读两本书或两本书以上 比如白手起家的百万富翁 马克 库班 (Mark Cuban)  和克利夫兰骑士队 (Cleveland Cavaliers) 老板丹·吉尔伯特 每天就会阅读一到三个小时 其实阅读的内容不需要难懂艰深 乔恩塞就建议 每个人可以在休息时间根据兴趣来阅读 这样你就可以吸收更多元的知识 查理自己经常会带着一本书 当我在等待会议开始前 或者坐车的时候都会阅读一两页 而不是浪费时间在智能手机上 如果你觉得很难执行 这里给大家提一个方法 就是每周给自己设定一个阅读目标 你可以每天读一章或者每月读一定数量的书 如今电子书广泛普及 无论你在哪里都可以进行几乎任何话题的阅读 比如比尔 盖茨 每年就会阅读大约 50 本不同领域的书籍 第二步是反思 反思是学习的一个关键部分 在不间断的学习过程中 一时间吸收太多信息会让你感到不知所措 这就好像你拿到了很多食材 但是没有沉下心思考要做什么菜 一堆好食材就被浪费了 所以 要从阅读中掌握新技能 反思是非常重要的 抽出时间对学习到的东西进行思考 可以让我们从中产生更多的领悟 和吸收新的观点 也能潜移默化的让我们积累想法 这样才能产生创造性的突破 德克萨斯大学的一项研究发现 精神休息和反思可以改善学习 专注于过去的经历 让我们有机会从犯过的错误中吸取教训 思考自己能否继续优化行为 进而制定更准确的目标计划 所以 谷歌有一个 20% 原则 让员工可以自由使用每周 20% 的工作时间 来放松或者做自己喜欢的事情 这样的放松不仅不会影响办公效率 反而可以激发更多的想象力和创造力 当然了 反思的时间和方式取决于你自己的安排 可以是在每天睡前将想法写在便利贴 并贴到自己专属的思考墙 又或者在一个人散步跑步时也可以进行思考 对查理来说 在每天的午餐之后 挤出 15 到 20 分钟来进行思考是比较理想的安排 因为我正从午饭后的低迷中走出来 可以通过沉思来让自己的脑细胞活跃起来 对于刚开始培养这个法则的人来说 可以从小处着手 每天就分配 5 到 10 分钟 然后慢慢的增加时间 这样你就不会在一开始时感到疲惫不想继续 同时 在思考的时候需要清楚自己想问的具体问题 注意 坚持一天只提出两到三个问题 并深入的思考这些问题的答案 例如  如果今天你参加了一个会议 那么在自己的反思时间时 你可以问自己 参加这个会议关键收获是什么？ 和我如何将这些知识应用到我的业务中？ 最后一个部分是快速实验 本杰明·富兰克林 (Benjamin Franklin) 和托马斯·爱迪生 (Thomas Edison) 正是因为他们的实验 而成为领先的发明家和思想家 实验如此有用 是因为它让我们看到事实而不是假设 也就是说 实验会向你展示什么是有效的 你可以从错误中学习 并从别人那里获得反馈 如果你想在生活中取得进步 实验是必不可少的 每周留出一些时间来测试新理论或想法 不管它们有多疯狂 世界上一些最成功的产品 比如马斯克的地下隧道就是实验的结果 创新永远不会来自一遍又一遍地做同样的事情 即使你的实验失败了 你也会学到宝贵的经验 所以 把阅读到的知识和理论实践于生活中 可以让你感觉到阅读的实际好处 也可以让你更加坚持阅读 其实实验没那么耗时间 大多数在你没意识到的时候 你已经把阅读的内容应用或实践于日常生活中 比如 在阅读了一本关于协作和团队合作的书后 你可以从事新的志愿者工作来运用这些知识 或者在阅读大量投资新闻和财报后 对有信心的公司进行长期定投 这些都是可以学习和验证自己想法的机会 人们很容易把工作和学习混为一谈 这就是为什么你最终会感到停滞不前 或许有人会觉得 我每周工作 40 小时 我肯定会有进步的 但事实并非如此 重复的工作会让你容易形成惰性思维 当你专注于每天一样的日常问题时 你没有给自己更多的时间和空间去发展和成长 对于很多人来说 他们通过完成的任务量来衡量自己的工作表现 因此 他们在一天中急速前进 却不愿意放慢脚步 提高自己的改进率 而五小时法则反其道而行之 它首先关注的是刻意的学习 而不是每天去工作的时候 希望自己能顺便从中学到什么东西 如果你一直专注于当前的工作 而不是长期的自我提升 你将永远看不到太多的发展 这就是为什么提出五小时法则的富兰克林认为 即使在那一天他会完成较少的事情 他也情愿花上至少一个小时进行学习 因为从长远来看 这可谓是他对自己的时间所做的最好投资 为了方便大家理解 查理给大家举个简单的例子 很多销售人员在打电话之前都不会做任何研究 他们直接拿起电话就打 打完了就继续拨打下一个电话 一天下来他们可以打很多通销售电话 甚至几百个  工作效率看似达到了 但他们从中收获了什么呢？ 答案可能是没有 不具有主动学习思维的人 不能从这些重复的电话中学习到什么东西 而一个具有学习关注点的人就会去想明白 在打电话时要练习的技巧 比如要怎么样才能让客户更信任我 不会想要立马挂电话？ 这样的人会在过程中进行练习 并反思从中取得的收获 如果他还想真正的进行更高水平的学习 他可以在打电话时邀请一位同事去听 并在之后请那位同事提出真实的反馈 当你具有主动学习的观念 并采取了这样的生活方式 这就意味着每打出一个销售电话 你就会在这方面有所改进 把焦点放在学习可以改掉我们的自动化行为 这样才能保持进步而非停滞不前 我们生活中的每件事情 都是一个用来提升自己的机会 有意识的学习才能让我们进步 而不是止步不前 通过聚焦于学习的生活方式 我们可以在长期完成更多的事情 每周给自己五个小时的学习时间 可能很难得到立竿见影的回报 但从长远来看 你会看到巨大的进步 给自己设定具体的学习目标 把时间花在成为最好的自己上 给自己时间去实现它们 这些经历慢慢积累 可以帮助你在通往成功的路上走得很好 曾有人总结大佬们的工作方式 关键不在于如何工作 而在于如何休息 无论有意无意 更多的企业家和业界精英们 在验证着这个 5 小时法则 希望它能在繁忙的日常中 帮你学到能够改变你人生的知识与技能 那么 你已经准备好开始执行五小时法则了吗？

videos.title: 公開我買過的全部股票：四年來究竟賠了多少錢？
videos.transcript: Hey! You're right According to a lot of comments I've been a scammer for 4 years. 這4年間呢 我總是以「害」我的觀眾輸錢為快樂 甚至看我穿著Grab的背心 就很多的指控說我收了Grab的錢 所以幫他們打廣告 哪一支股票下跌了 那鐵定就是我收了他的錢在幫他打廣告 So Now that I'm leaving, You guys deserve to know the TRUTH 所以今天 我會把過去四年我在自己的頻道 影片、直播、別的節目所有地方 我曾經講過的所有股票 一次列出來給你看 當時我說我買進的價格&現在的價格 Everything you need to know and this should put the conversation to sleep for good 因為過去4年 在投資上面我做了很多功課 付出了很多時間 做了很多正確的決定 買了很多好股票 當然也犯了很多錯誤 買了不少的爛股票 那今天這一份名單呢 或是說這份資料呢 就可以讓你自己判斷 Maybe I'm just a scammer lol Alright so without further ado guys Let's get the BGM rolling for one last time! 所以透過這份資料 應該很明確可以看到 過去4年我確實買入了不止一兩支 害我虧錢的爛股票 但你也可以很清楚看到 不管你相不相信這份資料的真實性 不管你要怎麼調整這份資料 讓他看起來更貼近現實 那事實就是 我們的投資組合過去4年 是毫無疑問擊敗大盤的 That is the "big picture" 這也是為什麼我常跟大家說 You've got to focus on the big picture 因為長期投資他的魅力就是 You are allowed to make mistakes and you will make mistakes and you will keep making mistakes 我這邊說的"you"可能是你，也可以是我 我以後也會繼續選到爛的股票啊 And so what? 所以這種東西 你可以說他是"Negativity Bias" 心理學叫做Negativity Bias 就是今天如果你發生了九件好事 但你發生了一件壞事 你就會覺得今天是個"Bad Day" and guess what? 如果你今天發生了九件壞事和一件好事呢？ It's an even worse day!!! 所以人們會傾向於 或者說人們就天生會去放大負面的東西 這就是我剛剛講的Negativity Bias 那如果長期投資跟主動選股 是你選擇的道路的話 Then you gotta deal with Negativity Bias 因為不可能你投資的這個生涯呢 都不犯任何一個錯誤 Nobody's perfect and you DON'T have to be perfect! 你如果覺得這個頻道 不能帶給你任何價值的話 Then so be it I'm sure there are other places that fits you more 但我不希望的是 你因為「負面偏誤」而懷疑自己 你可以不喜歡這個頻道的風格 這個頻道的投資組合 或者這個頻道分析的方式 但是如果你的投資組合跟我類似 Mostly you are picking winners 但你因為一兩個losers 讓你自己過意不去的話 那你其實沒有做好身為投資者 最重要的一個功課：Focus on the big picture  As an investor, you make mistakes; you learn and then you move on you don't stuck! You move on you focus on the big picture So yep that's pretty much all I got for you  I got one more video for you though:) 下一支影片會跟大家分享 我離開YouTube一陣子的原因是什麼 好啦如果你把影片看到這邊的話 我就想跟你說 Thank you for the support I appreciate you ; I love you and I hope you have a great day and I will see you in the next video
